next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

By the next morning, rain had stopped pretending it was weather and settled into the fabric of everything before Marisol got off the B61 at Van Brunt. Her cuffs were wet above the ankle. Her left sock had started to slide down inside her boot and bunch under her arch. In the lot outside the warehouse, puddles held diesel rainbows that never broke apart, no matter how hard the wind pushed them. She stood there a second with her tote pressed to her ribs, watching a forklift reverse into a loading bay, its warning beep flat and patient, like a hospital monitor that had stopped caring whether anyone lived.

The Red Hook annex sat three blocks from the water, a brick shell with patched windows and a roll gate that looked as if it had lost fights with three different storms and one union election. This was where everything the museum did not have room to remember ended up: busted pedestals, old registrarial binders, obsolete labels, transit crates with three layers of tape and no legible handwriting left. A paper sign on the side door read `RING BELL IF YOU HAVE BUSINESS`. Somebody had crossed out `BUSINESS` in marker and written `A WARRANT`.

Marisol rang anyway.

The woman who opened had silver glitter on two fingernails and a fleece vest with a chess-club patch stitched over the chest where a name tag had once been. She did not bother smiling.

"You museum?"

"Residency project," Marisol said. "I emailed yesterday. I'm looking for handling manifests tied to T.V. inventory tags. Late nineties through early two-thousands, if those are boxed by year."

The woman lean...
open_hooks_to_carry: Ada explains what conservation can prove and what it cannot.
Marisol sees scrape marks where a face was deliberately erased.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...t down and leaned against the cool painted cinderblock. She took out her phone and looked at the two photographs she had just taken. Front: Gloria and Salvador on a boardwalk, young enough to still believe the camera was harmless. Back: Noviembre 1989. After shift. G + S.

She zoomed in until the ink broke into pixels. She zoomed out until both of them fit in her palm.

For years she had held Salvador at a usable distance: the famous painter, the manipulator, the dying man with theatrics and morphine and a talent for other people’s weak spots. He had been external pressure, a storm moving in from somewhere else. The photo changed the geometry. He had stood in her mother’s weather before Marisol had language for weather at all.

Upstairs, someone laughed, then coughed, then dragged a chair across tile. Gloria’s apartment door opened and closed. Life resumed its normal rude volume around the secret like water around a submerged stone.

Marisol put the phone away. The images were contraband on a device her family borrowed all the time to check bus routes, school emails, and Leo's game settings. One swipe from Carina would detonate the secret before Marisol had enough proof to survive the blast. Gloria would call her a thief, Carina would cut off Leo, and every old photo in that apartment would disappear by morning.

She descended the rest of the stairs carrying leftover rice, coffee bread, and proof that she had crossed a line she could not uncross. Passive trust was over. At first light she would take the B41 back toward the museum, with evidence and fallout in equal measure.
state_deltas_required: Use conservation evidence to check whether Salvador altered visual history in his own work.
Infrared scans expose an overpainted male figure tagged "T.V.", moving the inquiry from rumor to traceable evidence.
